front: Composer: foremost Soviet composer after Shostakovich; Jewish Volga German ancestry; his music was suppressed by the Soviet government; gravestone has a fermata over a rest marked fff
Composer: coined 'polystylism' — mixing contrasting musical idioms from Baroque pastiche to tango; theorized in a 1971 Moscow lecture
Composer: wrote a Faust Cantata ending with a wild tango 'Es geschah' accompanying Faust's death; also wrote a ballet Peer Gynt
Composer: Symphony No. 3 uses an eight-tone row spelling out 'das Böse' ('the evil')
Composer: Third String Quartet opens with three explicitly-labeled quotations of Orlando di Lasso, Beethoven, and the DSCH motif
Composer: Quasi una sonata (Violin Sonata No. 2) — BACH motif in the passacaglia third movement; finale main theme based on 'La Cucaracha'
Concerto Grosso: Preludio first movement opens with an amplified piano prepared with kopeks (coins); the Rondo features a 'banal' E-flat minor tango on harpsichord
Concerto Grosso: Toccata movement features two solo violins in a frantic cascading stretto canon; commissioned by Gidon Kremer (1977)

Concerto: controversial polystylist cadenzas for this Beethoven work championed by Gidon Kremer; the first cadenza quotes Bartók and Shostakovich; another quotes Alban Berg; most soloists use Fritz Kreisler's cadenzas instead
Concerto: finished after the composer's first severe stroke; the coda is largely stratospheric and fortissimo, requiring the soloist to be amplified
